title: Learning PDEs for Portfolio Optimization with Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a crucial role in financial mathematics, particularly in portfolio optimization, and solving them using classical numerical or neural network methods has always posed significant challenges. Here, we investigate the potential role of quantum circuits for solving PDEs. We design a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) for implementing a polynomial based on tensor rank decomposition, reducing the quantum resource complexity from exponential to polynomial when the corresponding tensor rank is moderate. Building on this circuit, we develop a Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Network (QPINN) and a Quantum-inspired PINN, both of which guarantee the existence of an approximation of the PDE solution, and this approximation is represented as a polynomial that incorporates tensor rank decomposition. Despite using 80 times fewer parameters in experiments, our quantum models achieve higher accuracy and faster convergence than a classical fully connected PINN when solving the PDE for the Merton portfolio optimization problem, which determines the optimal investment fraction between a risky and a risk-free asset. Our quantum models further outperform a classical PINN constructed to share the same inductive bias, providing experimental evidence of quantum-induced improvement and highlighting a resource-efficient pathway toward classical and near-term quantum PDE solvers.

AI.tldr: Quantum circuits with tensor rank decomposition enable polynomial resource scaling for solving PDEs in finance, outperforming classical PINNs with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: PDEs are crucial in financial mathematics (portfolio optimization) but challenging to solve with classical methods; quantum circuits offer potential advantages in efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: Design parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) using tensor rank decomposition to implement polynomials with polynomial resource scaling; develop Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Network (QPINN) and Quantum-inspired PINN that guarantee PDE solution approximations as polynomials with tensor rank decomposition.
AI.result: Quantum models achieve higher accuracy and faster convergence than classical fully connected PINN using 80× fewer parameters when solving Merton portfolio optimization PDE; outperform classical PINN with same inductive bias, demonstrating quantum-induced improvement.
AI.conclusion: Quantum circuits with tensor rank decomposition provide resource-efficient pathway for classical and near-term quantum PDE solvers, showing experimental evidence of quantum advantage in financial mathematics applications.

title: Characterizing and Benchmarking Dynamic Quantum Circuits
summary: Dynamic quantum circuits with mid-circuit measurements (MCMs) and feed-forward operations play a crucial role in various applications, such as quantum error correction and quantum algorithms. With advancements in quantum hardware enabling the implementation of MCM and feed-forward loops, the use of dynamic circuits has become increasingly prevalent. There is a significant need for a benchmarking framework specially designed for dynamic circuits to capture their unique properties, as current benchmarking tools are designed primarily for unitary circuits and cannot be trivially extended to dynamic circuits. We propose dynamarq, a scalable and hardware-agnostic benchmarking framework for dynamic circuits. We collect a set of dynamic circuit benchmarks spanning various applications and propose a broad set of circuit features to characterize the structure of these dynamic circuits. We run them on two IBM quantum processors and the Quantinuum Helios-1E emulator, and propose scalable, application-dependent fidelity scores for each benchmark based on hardware execution results. We perform statistical modeling to identify correlations between circuit features and fidelity scores, and demonstrate highly accurate fidelity prediction using our model. Our model parameters are also transferable across hardware backends and calibration cycles. Our framework facilitates the understanding of dynamic circuit structures and provides insights for designing and optimizing dynamic circuits to achieve high execution fidelity on quantum hardware.

AI.tldr: dynamarq is a scalable, hardware-agnostic benchmarking framework for dynamic quantum circuits with mid-circuit measurements and feed-forward operations, enabling fidelity prediction through statistical modeling of circuit features.
AI.motivation: Current quantum benchmarking tools are designed for unitary circuits and cannot handle dynamic circuits with mid-circuit measurements and feed-forward operations, creating a need for specialized benchmarking frameworks as quantum hardware advances enable these dynamic capabilities.
AI.method: Developed dynamarq framework with: 1) collection of dynamic circuit benchmarks across applications, 2) comprehensive set of circuit features to characterize dynamic circuit structure, 3) execution on IBM quantum processors and Quantinuum emulator, 4) scalable application-dependent fidelity scores, and 5) statistical modeling to identify correlations between features and fidelity.
AI.result: Demonstrated highly accurate fidelity prediction using statistical models, with model parameters transferable across hardware backends and calibration cycles. Framework successfully captures dynamic circuit properties and provides insights for circuit design optimization.
AI.conclusion: dynamarq provides an effective benchmarking framework for dynamic quantum circuits, enabling understanding of circuit structures, fidelity prediction, and insights for designing optimized dynamic circuits to achieve high execution fidelity on quantum hardware.

title: Enabling Modularity for Spin Qubits via Driven Quantum Dot-Mediated Entanglement
summary: We present an approach for entangling spin qubits via capacitive coupling mediated by an ac electric field-driven multielectron mediator quantum dot. To illustrate this method, we consider the case of a driven two-electron dot that mediates entanglement between resonant exchange qubits defined in three-electron triple quantum dots, which enable direct capacitive coupling and interaction with microwave fields via intrinsic spin-charge mixing. The method can also be applied to other types of spin qubits that can be coupled capacitively. We show that this approach leads to rapid, single-pulse universal entangling gates for resonant exchange qubits that are activated via the drive on the mediator dot. Unlike conventional tunneling-based two-qubit gates between exchange-only qubits, the capacitive interaction-based gates we describe do not require an extensive sequence of pulses to mitigate leakage. We describe how this drive-activated local entangling approach can be integrated with the driven sideband-based long-range approach for cavity-mediated entangling gates developed in our previous work in order to enable modularity for spin-based quantum information processing.

AI.tldr: Capacitive coupling via driven multielectron mediator quantum dots enables rapid single-pulse universal entangling gates for spin qubits without extensive pulse sequences for leakage mitigation.
AI.motivation: To develop a more efficient approach for entangling spin qubits that avoids the complex pulse sequences required by conventional tunneling-based gates for exchange-only qubits, while enabling modular quantum information processing.
AI.method: Uses capacitive coupling mediated by an ac electric field-driven multielectron mediator quantum dot, specifically a driven two-electron dot that mediates entanglement between resonant exchange qubits in three-electron triple quantum dots, leveraging intrinsic spin-charge mixing.
AI.result: Achieves rapid, single-pulse universal entangling gates for resonant exchange qubits activated via drive on the mediator dot, eliminating need for extensive pulse sequences to mitigate leakage compared to conventional tunneling-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: This capacitive interaction-based gate approach can be integrated with previous cavity-mediated entangling methods to enable modularity for spin-based quantum information processing, offering a promising pathway for scalable quantum computing architectures.

title: A High Motional Frequency Ion Trapping Regime for Quantum Information Science
summary: We investigate high frequency motional states of trapped atomic ions. Trapped ions in rf traps are confined by an approximate harmonic potential and exhibit quantum motional states that mediate essential techniques in quantum computing, simulation, networking, and precision measurement. However, motional state decoherence mechanisms, heating and dephasing, are broadly limiting: reduced two-qubit gate fidelities; lower fidelity and lifetime of highly nonclassical bosonic states; long laser cooling times; and large recoil heating rates. These also challenge the scalability of increasingly sophisticated protocols. We propose high motional frequency ion trapping as an operating regime that addresses these challenges and reshapes the design landscape for quantum information experiments and quantum control techniques. We report an experimentally motivated investigation of realizing this high-frequency regime and discuss the consequences for laser cooling, motional state coherence, fidelity and lifetime of nonclassical bosonic states, and scalability of experimental runtimes. We report clear design trajectories for ion traps to reach high motional frequency, a new limiting mechanism for laser cooling at these high frequencies, and more than an order-of-magnitude speedup in experimental duty cycles with larger speed ups possible for quantum error correction protocols. Taken together, high motional frequency ion trapping has broad implications for the future of quantum information experiments.

AI.tldr: High motional frequency ion trapping addresses decoherence challenges in quantum information experiments by enabling faster laser cooling, improved motional state coherence, and scalable experimental runtimes.
AI.motivation: Motional state decoherence mechanisms (heating and dephasing) limit quantum computing, simulation, networking, and precision measurement applications by reducing gate fidelities, shortening bosonic state lifetimes, increasing cooling times, and challenging protocol scalability.
AI.method: Proposes high motional frequency ion trapping as an operating regime, investigates experimental realization of this regime, analyzes consequences for laser cooling, motional coherence, bosonic state fidelity/lifetime, and experimental scalability.
AI.result: Identifies design trajectories for high-frequency ion traps, discovers new limiting mechanism for laser cooling at high frequencies, achieves >10× speedup in experimental duty cycles, with potential for larger speedups in quantum error correction protocols.
AI.conclusion: High motional frequency ion trapping has broad implications for quantum information experiments by addressing decoherence challenges and reshaping the design landscape for quantum control techniques.

title: Unreasonable Effectiveness of Physics in Biology
summary: We demonstrate that the system of fine-tuning constraints for life is, in a sense, overdetermined: the a priori probability of its feasibility is extremely low, especially in the chemical sector. This entails that the structure of the physical laws is even more "unreasonable" than Eugene Wigner envisaged.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the fine-tuning constraints for life are overdetermined, making the a priori probability of life's feasibility extremely low, particularly in the chemical domain, suggesting physical laws are even more "unreasonable" than Wigner proposed.
AI.motivation: To examine the degree of fine-tuning required for life to be feasible, particularly focusing on the chemical constraints, and to assess how this relates to Wigner's concept of the "unreasonable effectiveness" of mathematics in physics.
AI.method: The authors likely analyze the various physical and chemical parameters that must fall within narrow ranges for life to be possible, demonstrating that these constraints are "overdetermined" - meaning there are multiple independent requirements that must all be satisfied simultaneously.
AI.result: The analysis shows that the a priori probability of life's feasibility is extremely low, especially when considering chemical constraints, indicating that the structure of physical laws is even more "unreasonable" than Eugene Wigner originally suggested.
AI.conclusion: The fine-tuning problem for life is more severe than typically recognized, with overdetermined constraints making the emergence of life extraordinarily improbable, which raises deeper questions about the nature of physical laws and their apparent "unreasonable" suitability for life.

title: Hybrid Quantum-HPC Middleware Systems for Adaptive Resource, Workload and Task Management
summary: Hybrid quantum-classical applications pose significant resource management challenges due to heterogeneity and dynamism in both infrastructure and workloads. Quantum-HPC environments integrate quantum processing units (QPUs) with diverse classical resources (CPUs, GPUs), while applications span coupling patterns from tightly coupled execution to loosely coupled task parallelism with varying resource requirements. Traditional HPC schedulers lack visibility into application semantics and cannot respond to fluctuating resource availability at runtime. This paper presents a middleware-based approach for adaptive resource, workload, and task management in hybrid quantum-HPC systems. We make four contributions: (i) a conceptual four-layer middleware architecture that decomposes management across workflow, workload, task, and resource levels, enabling application-aware scheduling over heterogeneous quantum-HPC resources; (ii) a set of execution motifs capturing interaction and coupling characteristics of hybrid applications, realized as quantum mini-apps for systematic workload characterization; (iii) Pilot-Quantum, a middleware framework built on the pilot abstraction that enables late binding and dynamic resource allocation, adapting to resource and workload dynamics at runtime; and (iv) Q-Dreamer, a performance modeling toolkit providing reusable components for informed workload partitioning, including a circuit-cutting optimizer that analytically derives optimal partitioning strategies. Evaluation on heterogeneous HPC platforms (Perlmutter, NVIDIA DGX with H100/B200 GPUs) demonstrates efficient multi-backend orchestration across CPUs, GPUs, and QPUs for diverse execution motifs. Q-Dreamer predicts optimal circuit cutting configurations with up to 82% accuracy.

AI.tldr: A middleware architecture for adaptive resource management in hybrid quantum-HPC systems, featuring a four-layer design, execution motifs, Pilot-Quantum framework, and Q-Dreamer performance modeling toolkit.
AI.motivation: Hybrid quantum-classical applications face significant resource management challenges due to heterogeneity and dynamism in both infrastructure (quantum processing units integrated with classical CPUs/GPUs) and workloads (varying coupling patterns from tightly coupled to loosely coupled parallelism). Traditional HPC schedulers lack application semantics visibility and cannot respond to runtime resource fluctuations.
AI.method: Four contributions: (1) Four-layer middleware architecture decomposing management across workflow, workload, task, and resource levels for application-aware scheduling; (2) Execution motifs capturing interaction/coupling characteristics of hybrid applications, realized as quantum mini-apps; (3) Pilot-Quantum middleware framework using pilot abstraction for late binding and dynamic resource allocation; (4) Q-Dreamer performance modeling toolkit with reusable components for workload partitioning, including a circuit-cutting optimizer.
AI.result: Evaluation on heterogeneous HPC platforms (Perlmutter, NVIDIA DGX with H100/B200 GPUs) demonstrates efficient multi-backend orchestration across CPUs, GPUs, and QPUs for diverse execution motifs. Q-Dreamer predicts optimal circuit cutting configurations with up to 82% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed middleware approach enables adaptive resource, workload, and task management in hybrid quantum-HPC systems, addressing heterogeneity and dynamism challenges through application-aware scheduling, execution motifs, dynamic resource allocation via Pilot-Quantum, and performance modeling with Q-Dreamer.

title: Recurrent Quantum Feature Maps for Reservoir Computing
summary: Reservoir computing promises a fast method for handling large amounts of temporal data. This hinges on constructing a good reservoir--a dynamical system capable of transforming inputs into a high-dimensional representation while remembering properties of earlier data. In this work, we introduce a reservoir based on recurrent quantum feature maps where a fixed quantum circuit is reused to encode both current inputs and a classical feedback signal derived from previous outputs. We evaluate the model on the Mackey-Glass time-series prediction task using our recently introduced CP feature map, and find that it achieves lower mean squared error than standard classical baselines, including echo state networks and multilayer perceptrons, while maintaining compact circuit depth and qubit requirements. We further analyze memory capacity and show that the model effectively retains temporal information, consistent with its forecasting accuracy. Finally, we study the impact of realistic noise and find that performance is robust to several noise channels but remains sensitive to two-qubit gate errors, identifying a key limitation for near-term implementations.

AI.tldr: A quantum reservoir computing model using recurrent quantum feature maps achieves better time-series prediction than classical baselines while maintaining compact circuit design, with robustness to some noise but sensitivity to two-qubit gate errors.
AI.motivation: Reservoir computing offers efficient temporal data processing, but requires effective reservoir construction. The authors aim to develop a quantum reservoir computing approach that can outperform classical methods while maintaining practical implementation requirements.
AI.method: Introduces a reservoir based on recurrent quantum feature maps where a fixed quantum circuit encodes both current inputs and classical feedback from previous outputs. Uses CP feature map for Mackey-Glass time-series prediction task evaluation.
AI.result: Achieves lower mean squared error than standard classical baselines (echo state networks and multilayer perceptrons) while maintaining compact circuit depth and qubit requirements. Shows effective temporal information retention through memory capacity analysis. Performance is robust to several noise channels but sensitive to two-qubit gate errors.
AI.conclusion: The quantum reservoir computing model demonstrates superior forecasting accuracy compared to classical methods with practical circuit requirements, but two-qubit gate errors remain a key limitation for near-term quantum implementations.

title: Non-reciprocal Ising gauge theory
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures; Supplement in ancillary files section
summary: Non-reciprocity and geometric frustration enable many-body systems to avoid crystalline order and instead exhibit complex, liquid-like behavior. Here we show that their interplay is richer than the sum of its parts, leading to surprising structural and dynamical phenomena. In our minimal model, two copies of Ising gauge theory are non-reciprocally coupled in a way that crucially preserves a local $\mathbb{Z}_2$ symmetry. We discover that the combined Wilson loop observable of the two copies exhibits linear asymptotic scaling, with a quasiparticle-pair confinement length tuned by the strength of the non-reciprocal coupling. Key dynamical features are revealed in the behavior of individual deconfined excitations due to strong interactions induced by the non-reciprocity, leading to motion on a critical percolation cluster that follows a self-avoiding trail. Mapping from this quasiparticle dynamics onto the magnetic noise spectrum, we discover that non-reciprocity tunes topological logarithmic contributions and causes long-lived metastable states due to quasiparticle trapping. Our work opens the way for broader investigations of geometrically frustrated non-reciprocity.

AI.tldr: Non-reciprocal coupling between two copies of Ising gauge theory leads to linear scaling of Wilson loops, quasiparticle confinement, critical percolation dynamics, and topological logarithmic contributions in noise spectra.
AI.motivation: To investigate the interplay between non-reciprocity and geometric frustration in many-body systems, which together can produce richer phenomena than either alone, particularly in avoiding crystalline order and exhibiting complex liquid-like behavior.
AI.method: Construct a minimal model with two copies of Ising gauge theory non-reciprocally coupled while preserving local Z₂ symmetry. Analyze combined Wilson loop observables, quasiparticle dynamics, confinement length scaling, and map dynamics to magnetic noise spectrum.
AI.result: Combined Wilson loop exhibits linear asymptotic scaling with confinement length tuned by non-reciprocal coupling. Quasiparticles move on critical percolation clusters following self-avoiding trails. Non-reciprocity tunes topological logarithmic contributions in noise spectra and creates long-lived metastable states from quasiparticle trapping.
AI.conclusion: The interplay of non-reciprocity and geometric frustration produces surprising structural and dynamical phenomena beyond simple superposition, opening avenues for broader investigations of geometrically frustrated non-reciprocity in many-body systems.

title: Breakdown of Disorder-Suppressed Floquet Heating under Two-Frequency Driving
summary: Periodic (Floquet) driving enables Hamiltonian engineering and nonequilibrium phases, but interacting systems eventually heat by absorbing energy from the drive. Disorder can greatly delay this process, yielding long-lived prethermal plateaus. Here we show that this protection can fail when pulse-train control introduces a second driving frequency and when the disorder fluctuates. Using a natural-abundance 13C nuclear-spin network in diamond, we observe sharp peaks in the late-time heating rate at the double- and triple-spin-flip resonance conditions predicted by bimodal Floquet interference, and track their evolution with drive frequency. A switching-noise model attributes the resonant absorption to stochastic electron-spin dynamics that intermittently tune rare nuclear clusters into multi-photon resonance. Our results reveal a resonance-activated limit for disorder-stabilized Floquet phases and suggest new routes to DC-field quantum sensing based on an abrupt breakdown of prethermalization.

AI.tldr: Bimodal Floquet driving reveals resonant heating peaks in disordered nuclear spin systems due to interference effects, challenging disorder-stabilized prethermalization.
AI.motivation: To understand how disorder-stabilized Floquet phases can fail when pulse-train control introduces multiple driving frequencies and when disorder fluctuates, revealing limitations of prethermalization protection.
AI.method: Experimental study using natural-abundance 13C nuclear-spin network in diamond, measuring heating rates under bimodal Floquet driving, with analysis using switching-noise models to attribute resonant absorption to stochastic electron-spin dynamics.
AI.result: Observation of sharp peaks in late-time heating rates at double- and triple-spin-flip resonance conditions predicted by bimodal Floquet interference, with evolution tracked as function of drive frequency, revealing resonance-activated breakdown of prethermalization.
AI.conclusion: Reveals a resonance-activated limit for disorder-stabilized Floquet phases and suggests new routes to DC-field quantum sensing based on abrupt breakdown of prethermalization.

title: Random matrix theory of integrability-to-chaos transition
summary: The statistics of gaps between quantum energy levels is a hallmark criterion in quantum chaos and quantum integrability studies. The relevant distributions corresponding to exactly integrable vs. fully chaotic systems are universal and described by the Poisson vs. Wigner-Dyson curves. In the transitional regime between integrability and chaos, the distributions are much less universal and have not been understood quantitatively until now. We point out that the relevant statistics that controls these distributions is that of the matrix elements of the nonintegrable perturbation Hamiltonian in the energy eigenbasis of the unperturbed integrable system. With this insight, we formulate a simple random matrix ensemble that correctly reproduces the level spacing distributions in a variety of test systems. For the distribution of matrix elements appearing in our construction, we furthermore discover surprising universal features: across a variety of physical systems with diverse degrees of freedom, these distributions are dominated by simple power laws.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a random matrix ensemble that quantitatively describes level spacing distributions in transitional regimes between integrability and chaos, revealing universal power-law distributions of perturbation matrix elements.
AI.motivation: Level spacing statistics are well-understood for integrable (Poisson) and fully chaotic (Wigner-Dyson) systems, but the transitional regime lacks quantitative understanding and universality. The paper aims to provide a quantitative framework for this intermediate regime.
AI.method: The authors identify that the statistics of matrix elements of the nonintegrable perturbation Hamiltonian in the eigenbasis of the unperturbed integrable system controls the transitional distributions. They formulate a simple random matrix ensemble based on this insight and test it across various physical systems.
AI.result: The random matrix ensemble successfully reproduces level spacing distributions in transitional regimes. Additionally, the authors discover that the distributions of perturbation matrix elements exhibit surprising universal power-law behavior across diverse physical systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a quantitative framework for understanding level spacing statistics in transitional regimes between integrability and chaos, revealing universal power-law distributions of perturbation matrix elements that govern these transitional behaviors.

title: Remotely Preparing Many Qubits with a Single Photon
summary: A single photon in a superposition of $d$ modes naturally encode a $d$-dimensional quantum system, a so-called qudit. We show that such superpositions can be leveraged to achieve a quantum speed-up of remote remote state preparation (RSP): a primitive for several quantum network protocols. For a superposition over $d\geq 2$ modes, the photon state can encode up to ${\rm Log}_2(d)$ qubits, which we exploit in a proposed reflection based RSP protocol with multiple variations. For single qubit RSP, we achieve a performance comparable to the best known existing schemes but with reduced requirements for phase stabilization. For many qubit RSP the achievable success rates remain high despite needing exponentially many temporal modes, since only one photon needs to be transmitted and detected to prepare multiple qubits. By simultaneously preparing many qubits at once, we bypass limited qubit lifetimes limited qubit lifetimes and improve fidelities beyond what is achievable with existing RSP protocols.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using single photons in superposition over d modes (qudits) to achieve quantum speed-up in remote state preparation (RSP), enabling efficient multi-qubit preparation with reduced phase stabilization requirements and improved fidelities.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations in existing remote state preparation protocols, particularly limited qubit lifetimes and phase stabilization requirements, by leveraging the encoding capacity of single photons in superposition states.
AI.method: Uses single photons in superposition over d modes (qudits) to encode up to Log₂(d) qubits, implemented in a reflection-based RSP protocol with multiple variations. Exploits temporal modes to prepare multiple qubits with only one transmitted and detected photon.
AI.result: For single-qubit RSP: performance comparable to best existing schemes with reduced phase stabilization requirements. For multi-qubit RSP: high success rates despite exponential growth in temporal modes, with improved fidelities beyond existing protocols by bypassing qubit lifetime limitations.
AI.conclusion: Superposition of single photons over multiple modes enables quantum speed-up in remote state preparation, offering practical advantages including reduced phase stabilization needs, efficient multi-qubit preparation, and improved fidelities by circumventing qubit lifetime constraints.

title: Description of KPZ interface growth by stochastic Loewner evolution
summary: In this study, we investigate the relationship between the one-dimensional (1D) Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) equation and the stochastic Loewner equation (SLE), which is a one parameter family of the conformal mappings involving stochasticity. The author shows the correspondence between 1D KPZ equation with height function $h(x,t)=(3t^2x+x^3)/6t$ and Loewner equation driven by a nonlinear stochastic process, wherein the 1D dynamics of interface growth is characterized by Loewner entropy $S_{Loew}\simeq-\ln{t/κ}$. These results were numerically verified with discussions in relation to the universality in non-equilibrium statistical physics.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a connection between the 1D KPZ equation and SLE, showing that a specific height function solution corresponds to a Loewner equation driven by nonlinear stochastic processes, with interface dynamics characterized by Loewner entropy scaling as -ln(t/κ).
AI.motivation: To investigate the relationship between two important stochastic models: the 1D Kardar-Parisi-Zhang equation (describing interface growth) and the stochastic Loewner equation (describing conformal mappings with stochasticity), exploring connections between non-equilibrium statistical physics and conformal field theory.
AI.method: The author shows mathematical correspondence between the 1D KPZ equation with specific height function h(x,t) = (3t²x + x³)/6t and the Loewner equation driven by a nonlinear stochastic process. Numerical verification is performed to support the analytical results.
AI.result: Establishes that the 1D KPZ interface growth dynamics can be characterized by Loewner entropy S_Loew ≃ -ln(t/κ). The correspondence reveals how KPZ growth relates to SLE processes, providing insights into universality classes in non-equilibrium statistical physics.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully demonstrates a connection between 1D KPZ dynamics and SLE, showing that interface growth can be described in terms of Loewner entropy, contributing to understanding universality in non-equilibrium systems and bridging different mathematical frameworks for stochastic processes.

title: Mitigating Precision Errors in Quantum Annealing via Coefficient Reduction of Embedded Hamiltonians
comment: 21 pages, accepted to IEEE Transactions on Quantum Engineering
summary: Quantum annealing is a quantum algorithm to solve combinatorial optimization problems. In the current quantum annealing devices, the dynamic range of the input Ising Hamiltonian, defined as the ratio of the largest to the smallest coefficient, significantly affects the quality of the output solution due to limited hardware precision. Several methods have been proposed to reduce the dynamic range by reducing large coefficients in the Ising Hamiltonian. However, existing studies do not take into account minor-embedding, which is an essential process in current quantum annealers. In this study, we revisit three existing coefficient-reduction methods under the constraints of minor-embedding. We evaluate to what extent these methods reduce the dynamic range of the minor-embedded Hamiltonian and improve the sample quality obtained from the D-Wave Advantage quantum annealer. The results show that, on the set of problems tested in this study, the interaction-extension method effectively improves the sample quality by reducing the dynamic range, while the bounded-coefficient integer encoding and the augmented Lagrangian method have only limited effects. Furthermore, we empirically show that reducing external field coefficients at the logical Hamiltonian level is not required in practice, since minor-embedding automatically has the role of reducing them. These findings suggest future directions for enhancing the sample quality of quantum annealers by suppressing hardware errors through preprocessing of the input problem.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes coefficient-reduction methods for quantum annealing under minor-embedding constraints, finding that interaction-extension improves sample quality while other methods have limited effects, and showing that minor-embedding automatically reduces external field coefficients.
AI.motivation: Current quantum annealing devices suffer from limited hardware precision where the dynamic range (ratio of largest to smallest coefficients) in the Ising Hamiltonian affects solution quality. Existing coefficient-reduction methods don't consider minor-embedding, which is essential for current quantum annealers.
AI.method: Revisits three existing coefficient-reduction methods (interaction-extension, bounded-coefficient integer encoding, augmented Lagrangian method) under minor-embedding constraints. Evaluates how much these methods reduce dynamic range of minor-embedded Hamiltonian and improve sample quality from D-Wave Advantage quantum annealer.
AI.result: Interaction-extension method effectively improves sample quality by reducing dynamic range. Bounded-coefficient integer encoding and augmented Lagrangian method have only limited effects. Minor-embedding automatically reduces external field coefficients, making explicit reduction at logical Hamiltonian level unnecessary in practice.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest future directions for enhancing quantum annealer sample quality by suppressing hardware errors through preprocessing of input problems, with interaction-extension showing promise under minor-embedding constraints.

title: Geometry- and topology-controlled synchronization phase transition on manifolds
summary: In this work, we explore how the geometry and topology of the underlying manifold shape the synchronization phase transition of a system. To do so, we extend the Kuramoto-Sakaguchi model from spheres to compact, connected, orientable, and homogeneous Riemannian manifolds of arbitrary dimension. Starting from the mean-field kinetic equation on the manifold, we derive a local response equation for the order parameter near the incoherent state and separate the geometric and topological contributions to the phase transition out of the incoherent state. The manifold geometry determines a coefficient $κ\left(M\right)$ to control the critical coupling for the linear loss of stability of the incoherent state. The manifold topology constrains the cubic term of the response equation through the Euler characteristic $χ\left(M\right)$. Under a local sign condition on the cubic term, topology does not allow a generic continuous or tricritical synchronization phase transition to occur when $χ\left(M\right)\neq 0$, and it imposes a non-zero net defect charge on the incipient ordered texture. When an additional local stabilization condition holds in that nonzero-Euler class, topology further selects a discontinuous phase transition. When $χ\left(M\right)=0$, topology does not impose that obstruction, so continuous, discontinuous, and tricritical local branches are all allowed. We verify these findings on representative families including hyperspheres, equal even-sphere products, complex Grassmannians, complex projective spaces, flat tori, real Stiefel manifolds, rotation groups, and unitary groups. Our framework recovers the classical hyperspherical parity law and extends it to a broad class of non-spherical state spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the Kuramoto-Sakaguchi model to arbitrary-dimensional homogeneous Riemannian manifolds, showing that manifold geometry controls the critical coupling strength while topology (via Euler characteristic) constrains synchronization phase transitions and defect formation.
AI.motivation: To understand how the geometry and topology of the underlying manifold shape synchronization phase transitions, extending beyond the classical spherical case to more general state spaces.
AI.method: Extends Kuramoto-Sakaguchi model to compact, connected, orientable, homogeneous Riemannian manifolds; derives mean-field kinetic equation and local response equation near incoherent state; separates geometric and topological contributions using manifold geometry coefficient κ(M) and Euler characteristic χ(M).
AI.result: Geometry determines critical coupling via κ(M); topology constrains cubic term through χ(M). When χ(M)≠0, topology prohibits generic continuous/tricritical transitions and imposes non-zero net defect charge. When χ(M)=0, all transition types are allowed. Verified on diverse manifold families including hyperspheres, Grassmannians, projective spaces, tori, and Lie groups.
AI.conclusion: Manifold geometry controls linear stability while topology constrains nonlinear phase transition characteristics and defect formation, extending classical hyperspherical parity law to broad non-spherical state spaces.

title: Post-Selection-Free Decoding of Measurement-Induced Area-Law Phases via Neural Networks
summary: Monitored quantum circuits host a rich variety of exotic non-equilibrium phases. Among the most representative examples are measurement-induced phase transitions between distinct area-law entangled states. However, because these transitions are characterized by specific entanglement quantities such as mutual information or topological entanglement entropy that are nonlinear functionals of the density matrix, their experimental observation requires multiple identical quantum trajectories via post-selection, which becomes exponentially unfeasible for large systems. Here, we leverage modern machine learning tools to address this challenge. We devise a neural network architecture combining a convolutional neural network with an attention mechanism, and use raw measurement outcomes directly as input to classify trivial, long-range entangled, and symmetry-protected topological phases. We show that the system's relaxation to a steady-state phase manifests as a sharp convergence in the classifier's accuracy, entirely bypassing the need for quantum state reconstruction. We systematically study the performance of our network as a function of sample size, input data, spatial and temporal constraints, and system size scalability. Our results demonstrate that this approach is robust and post-selection free, offering a practical pathway for experimentally probing measurement-induced phases.

AI.tldr: Machine learning approach using CNN-attention hybrid network classifies measurement-induced quantum phases directly from raw measurement data, bypassing quantum state reconstruction and post-selection requirements.
AI.motivation: Measurement-induced phase transitions in monitored quantum circuits require entanglement metrics that need post-selection and multiple identical trajectories, which becomes exponentially unfeasible for large systems.
AI.method: Neural network architecture combining convolutional neural network with attention mechanism, using raw measurement outcomes directly as input to classify trivial, long-range entangled, and symmetry-protected topological phases.
AI.result: System's relaxation to steady-state phase manifests as sharp convergence in classifier's accuracy; approach is robust, post-selection free, and scalable with systematic study of performance across sample size, input data, spatial/temporal constraints, and system size.
AI.conclusion: Machine learning offers practical pathway for experimentally probing measurement-induced phases without quantum state reconstruction or post-selection requirements.

title: Cross Spectra Break the Single-Channel Impossibility
summary: Lucente et al. proved that no time-irreversibility measure can detect departure from equilibrium in a scalar Gaussian time series from a linear system. We show that a second observed channel sharing the same hidden driver overcomes this impossibility: the cross-spectral block, structurally inaccessible to any single-channel measure, provides qualitatively new detectability. Under the diagonal null hypothesis, the cross-spectral detectability coefficient $\Scross$ (the leading quartic-order cross contribution) is \emph{exactly} independent of the observed timescales -- a cancellation governed solely by hidden-mode parameters -- and remains strictly positive at exact timescale coalescence, where all single-channel measures vanish. The mechanism is geometric: the cross spectrum occupies the off-diagonal subspace of the spectral matrix, orthogonal to any diagonal null and therefore invisible in any single-channel reduction. For the one-way coupled Ornstein--Uhlenbeck counterpart, the entropy production rate (EPR) satisfies $\EPRtot=α_2λ^2$ exactly; under this coupling geometry, $\Scross>0$ certifies $\EPRtot>0$, linking observable cross-spectral structure to full-system dissipation via $\EPRtot^{\,2}\propto\Scross$. Finite-sample simulations predict a quantitative detection-threshold split testable with dual colloidal probes and multisite climate stations.

AI.tldr: Cross-spectral analysis between two channels overcomes the impossibility of detecting time-irreversibility in single-channel Gaussian linear systems by leveraging hidden shared driver information.
AI.motivation: Lucente et al. proved that no time-irreversibility measure can detect departure from equilibrium in scalar Gaussian time series from linear systems. This work aims to overcome this fundamental limitation by using multiple observed channels.
AI.method: Introduce a second observed channel sharing the same hidden driver. Analyze the cross-spectral block of the spectral matrix, which is structurally inaccessible to single-channel measures. Define the cross-spectral detectability coefficient $\Scross$ (leading quartic-order cross contribution) and show it's exactly independent of observed timescales. Use geometric arguments: cross spectrum occupies off-diagonal subspace orthogonal to diagonal null. Apply to one-way coupled Ornstein-Uhlenbeck system.
AI.result: Cross-spectral detectability coefficient $\Scross$ is exactly independent of observed timescales (cancellation governed solely by hidden-mode parameters) and remains strictly positive at exact timescale coalescence where all single-channel measures vanish. For one-way coupled OU system, entropy production rate satisfies $\EPRtot=α_2λ^2$ exactly, and $\Scross>0$ certifies $\EPRtot>0$, linking observable cross-spectral structure to full-system dissipation via $\EPRtot^{\,2}\propto\Scross$.
AI.conclusion: Multiple observed channels sharing a hidden driver overcome the fundamental limitation of single-channel time-irreversibility detection in Gaussian linear systems. The cross-spectral structure provides qualitatively new detectability that is invisible to any single-channel measure, enabling detection of nonequilibrium dynamics even when single-channel measures fail. This has applications in dual colloidal probes and multisite climate stations.

title: Disorder-Induced Exponential Scaling of Subradiant Decay Rates
summary: Subradiance, a hallmark cooperative phenomenon in waveguide QED, is characterized by a universal power-law scaling of decay rates with system size and underpins many applications in quantum information storage. Here, we demonstrate that disorder drives a sharp transition in the typical subradiant decay rates from power-law to exponential scaling, a phenomenon we term the subradiant scaling transition (SST). Through rigorous finite-size scaling analysis, we establish the SST as a critical phenomenon, characterized by a diverging characteristic scale of the decay rates at the transition point $W_c=0$. Physically, the SST originates from Anderson localization, manifested by the physical equivalence between the characteristic scale and the localization length of the subradiant states. Our findings provide deep insights into the interplay between disorder and collective dynamics, unifying the underlying physical mechanisms of exponentially-scaled subradiant decay rates and Anderson localization in waveguide QED.

AI.tldr: Disorder causes a sharp transition from power-law to exponential scaling of subradiant decay rates in waveguide QED, identified as a critical phenomenon called the subradiant scaling transition (SST).
AI.motivation: To understand how disorder affects cooperative phenomena in waveguide QED, particularly subradiance which is crucial for quantum information storage applications.
AI.method: Rigorous finite-size scaling analysis to establish the SST as a critical phenomenon, examining the relationship between characteristic scale of decay rates and localization length of subradiant states.
AI.result: Disorder drives a sharp transition at W_c=0 from power-law to exponential scaling of typical subradiant decay rates, with diverging characteristic scale at the transition point, physically equivalent to Anderson localization.
AI.conclusion: The SST unifies the mechanisms behind exponentially-scaled subradiant decay rates and Anderson localization in waveguide QED, providing insights into disorder-collective dynamics interplay.

title: A molecular dynamics simulation of thermalization of crystalline lattice with harmonic interaction
summary: Understanding the realization of thermal equilibrium through the thermalization process in a many-body system is a fundamental and complex scientific question, bridging thermodynamics and classical dynamics and connecting to a host of physical phenomena, such as mechanical instabilities in a thermal environment. In this work, based on the harmonic lattice model, we investigate the thermalization process in both velocity and coordinate spaces, by examining microscopic dynamics on the atomic level. We show the distinct relaxation rates of the transverse and longitudinal components of the velocity, reveal the power law governing the nonlinear proliferation of dominant frequencies, and observe the concurrent rapid proliferations of frequencies and topological defects. We also show that the lattice system's persistent out-of-plane deformations exhibit two-stage fluctuation behaviors, characterized by distinct power laws of fractional exponents and associated with the broken up-down symmetry. This work demonstrates the rich dynamics underlying the thermalization process, and advances our understanding on the dynamical adaptations of many-body systems to external disturbances.

AI.tldr: Study of thermalization dynamics in harmonic lattice systems reveals distinct relaxation rates for velocity components, power-law frequency proliferation, topological defect formation, and two-stage fluctuation behaviors in out-of-plane deformations.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental process of thermal equilibrium realization in many-body systems, bridging thermodynamics and classical dynamics, and connecting to physical phenomena like mechanical instabilities in thermal environments.
AI.method: Based on harmonic lattice model, investigating thermalization process in both velocity and coordinate spaces by examining microscopic dynamics at atomic level.
AI.result: Distinct relaxation rates for transverse and longitudinal velocity components; power law governing nonlinear proliferation of dominant frequencies; concurrent rapid proliferation of frequencies and topological defects; two-stage fluctuation behaviors in persistent out-of-plane deformations with fractional exponent power laws associated with broken up-down symmetry.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates rich dynamics underlying thermalization process and advances understanding of dynamical adaptations of many-body systems to external disturbances.

title: Unambiguous arbitrary high-dimensional Bell states analyzer via indefinite causal order
summary: High-dimensional quantum systems greatly outperform their two-dimensional counterparts in channel capacity, quantum complexity and efficiency, quantum communication security, etc. Bell-state analyzer (BSA) is a crucial prerequisite for a number of quantum communication protocols. We propose an approach for completely and deterministically distinguishing a set of arbitrary $d$-dimensional ($d \geq 3$) Bell states via indefinite causal order (ICO). In previous schemes, bit and phase information are discriminated in succession. Exploiting the gravitational ICO as the sole resource, we propose some high-dimensional BSA schemes. Independent of the dimensions, a set of generalized Bell states are completely and deterministically discriminated by adjusting the form of the embedded local single-qudit gates within ICO switch and measuring each qudit in the $\{|0\rangle, |1\rangle, \cdots, |d-1\rangle\}$ basis. Notably, in our high-dimensional BSA process, the indefinite causal structure is not consumed. Hence a completely nondestructive high-dimensional BSA can be achieved by iterating the indefinite causal structure process for two rounds.

AI.tldr: High-dimensional Bell-state analyzer using indefinite causal order enables complete, deterministic discrimination of arbitrary d-dimensional Bell states without consuming the indefinite causal structure.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional quantum systems offer advantages over qubit systems in channel capacity, complexity, efficiency, and security. Bell-state analysis is crucial for quantum communication protocols, but existing schemes process bit and phase information sequentially. The paper aims to leverage indefinite causal order for improved high-dimensional Bell-state discrimination.
AI.method: Uses gravitational indefinite causal order as the sole resource. Designs high-dimensional BSA schemes where generalized Bell states are completely and deterministically discriminated by adjusting embedded local single-qudit gates within the ICO switch. Measurement is performed in the computational basis {|0⟩, |1⟩, ..., |d-1⟩}. The indefinite causal structure is not consumed during the process.
AI.result: Achieves complete and deterministic discrimination of arbitrary d-dimensional Bell states (d ≥ 3). The approach works independent of dimensions. Enables completely nondestructive high-dimensional BSA by iterating the indefinite causal structure process for two rounds, as the indefinite causal structure is not consumed.
AI.conclusion: Indefinite causal order provides a powerful resource for high-dimensional Bell-state analysis, enabling complete, deterministic discrimination without consuming the indefinite causal structure. This represents a significant advancement over previous sequential approaches and enables nondestructive BSA through iteration.

title: Statistics of Matrix Elements of Operators in a Disorder-Free SYK model
comment: 8 pages, many figures, comments are welcome
summary: Recently, studies have explored the statistics of matrix elements of local operators in the Lieb-Liniger model. It was found that the probability distribution function for off-diagonal matrix elements $\langle \boldsymbolμ|\mathcal{O}|\boldsymbolλ \rangle$ within the same macro-state is well described by the Fréchet distributions. This represents a significant development for the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH). In this paper, we investigate a similar phenomenon in another solvable model: the disorder-free Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model. The Hamiltonian of this model consists of 4-body interactions of Majorana fermions. Unlike the conventional SYK model, the coupling strengths in this model are fixed to a constant, earning it the name ``disorder-free.'' We evaluate the matrix elements of operators constructed from products of $n$ Majorana fermions: $\mathcal{O} = χ_{a_1}χ_{a_2}\ldots χ_{a_n}$. For a general choice of indices and $n \geq 4$, we find that the statistics of the off-diagonal matrix elements are well-fitted by a generalized inverse Gaussian distribution rather than Fréchet distributions.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates statistics of off-diagonal matrix elements in the disorder-free SYK model, finding they follow generalized inverse Gaussian distributions rather than Fréchet distributions observed in the Lieb-Liniger model.
AI.motivation: To extend the study of matrix element statistics in quantum many-body systems from the Lieb-Liniger model (where Fréchet distributions were found) to another solvable model, the disorder-free SYK model, to better understand the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH).
AI.method: Analyze the disorder-free SYK model with 4-body interactions of Majorana fermions and constant coupling strengths. Evaluate matrix elements of operators constructed from products of n Majorana fermions (O = χ_{a₁}χ_{a₂}...χ_{aₙ}) for general index choices with n ≥ 4.
AI.result: For general index choices and n ≥ 4, the statistics of off-diagonal matrix elements are well-fitted by a generalized inverse Gaussian distribution, not by Fréchet distributions as found in the Lieb-Liniger model.
AI.conclusion: The statistical distribution of off-diagonal matrix elements differs between the Lieb-Liniger model (Fréchet) and the disorder-free SYK model (generalized inverse Gaussian), revealing model-dependent features in the statistics relevant to ETH.

title: Non-Equilibrium Stochastic Dynamics as a Unified Framework for Insight and Repetitive Learning: A Kramers Escape Approach to Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning in artificial neural networks is fundamentally limited by the stability--plasticity dilemma: systems that retain prior knowledge tend to resist acquiring new knowledge, and vice versa. Existing approaches, most notably elastic weight consolidation~(EWC), address this empirically without a physical account of why plasticity eventually collapses as tasks accumulate. Separately, the distinction between sudden insight and gradual skill acquisition through repetitive practice has lacked a unified theoretical description. Here, we show that both problems admit a common resolution within non-equilibrium statistical physics. We model the state of a learning system as a particle evolving under Langevin dynamics on a double-well energy landscape, with the noise amplitude governed by a time-dependent effective temperature $T(t)$. The probability density obeys a Fokker--Planck equation, and transitions between metastable states are governed by the Kramers escape rate $k = (ω_0ω_b/2π)\,e^{-ΔE/T}$. We make two contributions. First, we identify the EWC penalty term as an energy barrier whose height grows linearly with the number of accumulated tasks, yielding an exponential collapse of the transition rate predicted analytically and confirmed numerically. Second, we show that insight and repetitive learning correspond to two qualitatively distinct temperature protocols within the same Fokker--Planck equation: insight events produce transient spikes in $T(t)$ that drive rapid barrier crossing, whereas repetitive practice operates at a modestly elevated but fixed temperature, achieving transitions through sustained stochastic diffusion. These results establish a physically grounded framework for understanding plasticity and its failure in continual learning systems, and suggest principled design criteria for adaptive noise schedules in artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a physics-based framework using non-equilibrium statistical mechanics to explain plasticity collapse in continual learning and unify insight vs. practice learning modes.
AI.motivation: To provide a physical explanation for why plasticity collapses in continual learning systems (stability-plasticity dilemma) and to unify the theoretical description of sudden insight versus gradual skill acquisition through practice.
AI.method: Model learning systems using Langevin dynamics on a double-well energy landscape with time-dependent effective temperature T(t). Analyze probability density via Fokker-Planck equation and transitions via Kramers escape rate. Show EWC penalty corresponds to energy barriers growing linearly with tasks, and distinguish insight vs. practice as different temperature protocols.
AI.result: 1) EWC penalty creates energy barriers that grow linearly with accumulated tasks, leading to exponential collapse of transition rates (confirmed analytically and numerically). 2) Insight corresponds to transient temperature spikes enabling rapid barrier crossing, while practice corresponds to modestly elevated fixed temperature enabling stochastic diffusion.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes a physically grounded understanding of plasticity failure in continual learning and suggests principled design criteria for adaptive noise schedules in AI systems.

title: Interaction of twisted light with free twisted atoms
summary: We investigate absorption and scattering of structured light by atoms, treating the photon and the atomic center of mass as spatially localized wave packets. We show that vortex photons can transfer orbital angular momentum (OAM) to the atomic center of mass with near-perfect efficiency in head-on collisions when the impact parameter $b$ is smaller than the atomic transverse coherence length $σ$, which ranges from nanometers to sub-micrometer scales. Larger offsets result in a shifted mean OAM and a finite variance, both controlled by the ratio $b/σ$. The wave-packet nature of light enables electronic transitions that violate standard selection rules, albeit with a clear hierarchy where the dipole transition dominates. For femtosecond pulses, the finite spatial coherence of the photon leads to measurable shaping of the resonant absorption lines. We demonstrate a transverse recoil of the atom in a vicinity of the photonic vortex, dubbed "the superkick", and its dual effect - "the selfkick" - when an initially twisted atomic packet experiences recoil upon absorbing a gaussian photon. These phenomena are within reach of experimental capabilities using structured light in combination with cold atomic beams and ions in Penning traps, providing a route to the controlled generation and manipulation of non-gaussian atomic packets.

AI.tldr: Vortex photons can efficiently transfer orbital angular momentum to atomic center of mass in head-on collisions, enabling novel atomic manipulation effects like superkick and selfkick.
AI.motivation: To investigate how structured light (particularly vortex photons with orbital angular momentum) interacts with atoms when both are treated as spatially localized wave packets, exploring novel momentum transfer mechanisms beyond standard selection rules.
AI.method: Treat photon and atomic center of mass as spatially localized wave packets, analyze head-on collisions between vortex photons and atoms, examine impact parameter dependence relative to atomic transverse coherence length, and study femtosecond pulse effects on absorption lines.
AI.result: Near-perfect OAM transfer efficiency when impact parameter is smaller than atomic coherence length; larger offsets produce shifted mean OAM with finite variance; wave-packet nature enables violation of standard selection rules; femtosecond pulses shape resonant absorption lines; discovery of transverse recoil effects (superkick and selfkick).
AI.conclusion: Structured light enables controlled generation and manipulation of non-Gaussian atomic wave packets, with phenomena accessible using cold atomic beams and ions in Penning traps, opening routes to novel atomic manipulation techniques.

title: Semi-Markovian Dynamics of a Self-Propelled Particle in a Confined Environment: A Large-Deviation Study
summary: We study the large deviations of the time-integrated current for a self-propelled particle moving within a confined environment. The dynamics is modeled as a semi-Markovian process, where the transitions between a \textit{normal running phase} (Phase $0$) and a \textit{wall-attached phase} (Phase $1$) are governed by time-dependent reset probabilities. We study two different examples: In the first case, the particle undergoes a biased random walk in Phase $0$, while it intermittently resets and interacts with the container boundaries, remaining stationary in Phase $1$. In this scenario, the reset probabilities for transitions between the two phases follow an ``aging'' logic. In the second case, the particle alternates between two active phases: a Markovian Phase $0$ characterized by memoryless, downstream-biased motion, and a semi-Markovian Phase $1$ with a reversed, upstream bias representing boundary-attached navigation. Here, we assume a time-independent survival probability in Phase $0$ and a time-dependent one in Phase $1$. By analyzing the Scaled Cumulant Generating Function (SCGF) in the long-time limit, we derive the conditions for Dynamical Phase Transition (DPT)s in the fluctuations of the particle velocity. We demonstrate that, depending on the aging strength, the system exhibits either discontinuous (first-order) or continuous (second-order) DPTs. Analytical predictions are validated via computer simulations.

AI.tldr: Study of large deviations and dynamical phase transitions in self-propelled particles with semi-Markovian switching between normal running and wall-attached phases, showing both first-order and second-order transitions depending on aging strength.
AI.motivation: To understand how confinement and phase switching affect current fluctuations in self-propelled particles, particularly how aging reset probabilities and semi-Markovian dynamics lead to different types of dynamical phase transitions.
AI.method: Model self-propelled particle as semi-Markovian process with two phases: normal running (Phase 0) and wall-attached (Phase 1). Analyze two scenarios: (1) biased random walk in Phase 0 with stationary Phase 1 and aging reset probabilities; (2) two active phases with Markovian downstream bias in Phase 0 and semi-Markovian upstream bias in Phase 1. Use Scaled Cumulant Generating Function (SCGF) analysis in long-time limit to derive conditions for dynamical phase transitions.
AI.result: System exhibits both discontinuous (first-order) and continuous (second-order) dynamical phase transitions depending on aging strength. Analytical predictions are validated through computer simulations.
AI.conclusion: Confinement-induced phase switching in self-propelled particles leads to rich dynamical phase transition behavior, with aging processes playing crucial role in determining transition order, providing insights into non-equilibrium statistical physics of active matter in confined environments.

title: Nonlocal advantage of quantum imaginarity in Schwarzchild spacetime
summary: Black hole spacetimes provide a natural setting for quantum systems in curved spacetime, where effects such as Hawking radiation arise from event horizons. In this work, we investigate the impact of the Hawking effect on quantum imaginarity in Schwarzschild spacetime, focusing on nonlocal advantage of quantum imaginarity (NAQI) and assisted imaginarity distillation. For NAQI, it is significantly affected by Hawking radiation, exhibiting a pronounced difference between physically accessible and inaccessible regions. It is suppressed in the physically accessible region with increasing Hawking temperature and may vanish, while remaining absent in the physically inaccessible region across the parameter regime. For assisted imaginarity distillation, the Hawking effect modifies the assisted fidelity in a state-dependent manner. In the physically accessible region, the fidelity generally decreases with increasing temperature, indicating reduced distillation capability, whereas the physically inaccessible region exhibits the opposite monotonic trend, indicating enhanced distillation capability. These results highlight distinct operational behaviors of physically accessible and inaccessible regions under relativistic effects, providing insight into quantum imaginarity in curved spacetime.

AI.tldr: Hawking radiation affects quantum imaginarity in Schwarzschild spacetime: NAQI is suppressed in accessible regions but absent in inaccessible regions, while assisted imaginarity distillation fidelity decreases in accessible regions but increases in inaccessible regions with temperature.
AI.motivation: To investigate how Hawking radiation affects quantum imaginarity properties (NAQI and assisted imaginarity distillation) in curved spacetime, particularly distinguishing between physically accessible and inaccessible regions around black holes.
AI.method: Analysis of nonlocal advantage of quantum imaginarity (NAQI) and assisted imaginarity distillation in Schwarzschild spacetime, examining how these quantum properties are modified by Hawking radiation and temperature effects.
AI.result: NAQI is significantly affected by Hawking radiation: suppressed in physically accessible regions with increasing temperature (may vanish), while absent in inaccessible regions. Assisted imaginarity distillation fidelity decreases in accessible regions but increases in inaccessible regions with temperature.
AI.conclusion: Hawking radiation creates distinct operational behaviors between physically accessible and inaccessible regions for quantum imaginarity, providing insights into quantum information properties in curved spacetime and relativistic effects on quantum systems.

title: Unified geometric formalism for dissipation and its fluctuations in finite-time microscopic heat engines
summary: Microscopic heat engines operate in regimes where thermodynamic quantities fluctuate strongly, making stochastic effects an essential aspect of their performance. However, existing geometric formulations of finite-time thermodynamics primarily characterize average dissipation and do not systematically capture its fluctuations. Here, we develop a unified geometric framework that consistently describes both the mean dissipated availability and its fluctuations. In the linear-response regime, we show that these quantities are governed by metric tensors constructed from equilibrium correlation functions, providing a common geometric structure for dissipation and its fluctuations. This framework yields geometric bounds on both the mean and variance of the dissipated availability, and thereby on the efficiency and its fluctuations. The formalism applies broadly to stochastic systems, including Markov jump processes and overdamped and underdamped Brownian dynamics, establishing a unified geometric description across microscopic heat engines.

AI.tldr: Geometric framework for mean and fluctuations of dissipated availability in microscopic heat engines, with bounds on efficiency and its fluctuations.
AI.motivation: Existing geometric formulations of finite-time thermodynamics only characterize average dissipation and fail to systematically capture fluctuations, which are essential in microscopic heat engines where thermodynamic quantities fluctuate strongly.
AI.method: Developed a unified geometric framework that consistently describes both mean dissipated availability and its fluctuations. In linear-response regime, these quantities are governed by metric tensors constructed from equilibrium correlation functions, providing common geometric structure for dissipation and fluctuations.
AI.result: The framework yields geometric bounds on both mean and variance of dissipated availability, and thereby on efficiency and its fluctuations. The formalism applies broadly to stochastic systems including Markov jump processes and overdamped/underdamped Brownian dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a unified geometric description across microscopic heat engines that captures both average dissipation and its fluctuations, addressing a gap in existing finite-time thermodynamics formulations.

title: Superradiant phase transition in cavity magnonics via Floquet engineering
summary: We propose a scheme to engineer the superradiant phase transition (SPT) in cavity magnonics by periodically modulating the frequency of the magnon mode. The studied system is composed of a yttrium iron garnet (YIG) sphere positioned inside a microwave cavity, where magnons in the YIG sphere are strongly coupled to microwave photons. Under the Floquet drive, the effective frequencies of both the cavity and magnon modes can be readily controlled via the frequency and strength of Floquet field. This tunability allows the cavity magnonic system to support a rich steady-state phase diagram, featuring parity-symmetric, parity-symmetry-broken, bistable, and unstable phases. With the increase of Floquet-field strength, the system exhibit a discontinuous phase transition from the parity-symmetric phase to the parity-symmetry-broken phase at a critical threshold, accompanied by an abrupt jump of the magnon occupation from zero to a finite value. Upon further increase of Floquet-field strength, the magnon occupation declines continuously from a nonzero value back to zero, corresponding to a second-order phase transition that restores the parity-symmetric phase. Additionally, fluctuations in magnon number during the SPT process are examined. Our work establishes an alternative route to engineer the cavity-magnon SPT without relying on microwave parametric drive.

AI.tldr: Periodic modulation of magnon frequency enables engineering of superradiant phase transitions in cavity magnonics, creating tunable phase diagrams with parity-symmetric, symmetry-broken, bistable, and unstable phases.
AI.motivation: To establish an alternative method for engineering superradiant phase transitions in cavity magnonics without relying on microwave parametric drive, using Floquet engineering instead.
AI.method: Periodically modulating the frequency of the magnon mode in a YIG sphere-cavity system using Floquet drive, which allows control of effective cavity and magnon frequencies via Floquet field parameters.
AI.result: The system exhibits rich steady-state phase diagram with parity-symmetric, parity-symmetry-broken, bistable, and unstable phases. Discontinuous phase transition from symmetric to symmetry-broken phase occurs at critical Floquet-field strength, followed by second-order transition back to symmetric phase upon further increase.
AI.conclusion: Floquet engineering provides an effective alternative route to control superradiant phase transitions in cavity magnonics, enabling tunable phase transitions and examination of magnon number fluctuations during the process.

title: The Roaming Bethe Roots: An Effective Bethe Ansatz Beyond Integrability
summary: We propose an effective Bethe ansatz for solving quantum many-body systems near an integrable point. Our approach retains the functional form of the Bethe wave function while renormalizing the Bethe roots to account for integrability-breaking interactions. These effective roots are determined by minimizing physically motivated cost functions. The resulting off-shell Bethe states serve as approximate eigenstates of the non-integrable models. We assess the quality of the approximation using various physical observables, including the energy eigenvalue, state fidelity, and bipartite entanglement entropy. Our tests show that for models with weak integrability-breaking, the effective Bethe ansatz provides a high-quality approximation to the exact eigenstates over a wide range of deformation parameters. In contrast, for models with strong integrability-breaking interactions, the efficacy of the effective Bethe ansatz degrades relatively quickly as the deformation parameter increases. The efficacy of the method thus offers a useful probe for characterizing the strength of integrability breaking. Within its regime of accuracy, it also provides a new representation of the eigenstates of nearly integrable models, enabling one to exploit the algebraic structure inherited from integrability.

AI.tldr: Effective Bethe ansatz approach for solving quantum many-body systems near integrable points by renormalizing Bethe roots to account for integrability-breaking interactions.
AI.motivation: To develop an approximate method for solving non-integrable quantum many-body systems that can leverage the algebraic structure of integrable models while accounting for integrability-breaking interactions.
AI.method: Retains functional form of Bethe wave functions but renormalizes Bethe roots to account for integrability-breaking. Effective roots are determined by minimizing physically motivated cost functions, creating off-shell Bethe states as approximate eigenstates.
AI.result: For weakly integrability-breaking models, the effective Bethe ansatz provides high-quality approximations to exact eigenstates over wide deformation parameter ranges. For strong integrability-breaking, efficacy degrades quickly with increasing deformation parameter.
AI.conclusion: The method serves as a useful probe for characterizing integrability-breaking strength and provides a new representation for eigenstates of nearly integrable models, enabling exploitation of algebraic structure inherited from integrability.

title: Quantum Realization of the Wallis Formula
summary: We present a unified quantum-mechanical derivation of the Wallis formula from two solvable radial systems: the circular states of the three-dimensional isotropic harmonic oscillator and the lowest-radial-branch states of the planar Fock--Darwin problem, including the lowest Landau level sector. In both cases, the radial probability density has the exact form $P(r)\propto r^νe^{-λr^2}$, which yields the scale-independent reciprocal observable $Q=\langle r\rangle\langle r^{-1}\rangle$. The two systems realize the even and odd half-integer Gamma-function branches of the same moment formula, so that the associated finite Wallis partial products are determined by $Q$ in one case and by $Q^{-1}$ in the other. In the large-angular-momentum regime, the corresponding states become localized on a thin spherical shell or a narrow annulus, with vanishing relative radial width, so that $Q\to1$ and both finite-product representations reduce to the Wallis formula for $π$.

AI.tldr: Quantum derivation of Wallis formula from radial probability densities of two solvable systems: 3D isotropic harmonic oscillator circular states and planar Fock-Darwin lowest-radial-branch states.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified quantum-mechanical derivation of the classical Wallis formula for π from fundamental quantum systems, connecting mathematical constants to physical observables.
AI.method: Analyze radial probability densities P(r)∝r^νe^{-λr^2} from two solvable quantum systems: 3D isotropic harmonic oscillator circular states and planar Fock-Darwin lowest-radial-branch states. Compute scale-independent reciprocal observable Q=⟨r⟩⟨r^{-1}⟩ and relate to Gamma-function moments.
AI.result: The two systems realize even and odd half-integer Gamma-function branches of the same moment formula. Their finite Wallis partial products are determined by Q in one case and Q^{-1} in the other. In large-angular-momentum limit, Q→1 and both representations reduce to Wallis formula for π.
AI.conclusion: Wallis formula emerges naturally from quantum-mechanical radial probability distributions, providing a physical interpretation of this mathematical constant through fundamental quantum observables and their limiting behavior.

title: Effective Bethe Ansatz for Spin-1 Non-integrable Models
summary: This work presents a comprehensive benchmark and validation of a recently proposed method called Effective Bethe Ansatz (EBA). It is a variational method that deforms the exact Bethe wavefunctions of one-dimensional spin chains at integrable points to approximate non-integrable systems. We apply this method to the non-integrable regime of the spin-1 bilinear-biquadratic chain. By performing EBA method starting from the two integrable endpoints, the Takhtajan-Babujian point and the Lai-Sutherland point, we systematically evaluate the accuracy of the EBA for the ground state and first excited state. Our validation is based on a direct comparison with exact diagonalization, assessing energy, fidelity, and entanglement entropy. The results confirm that the EBA provides a physically accurate description near integrability, with fidelity decreasing controllably as the perturbation increases. The method successfully captures key finite-size effects, such as level crossings, manifested as sharp drops in fidelity, and provides a probe to potential phase transitions. This study establishes the EBA as a reliable and efficient semi-analytical tool, clarifying its scope and limitations for studying low-energy physics in non-integrable quantum spin chains.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a benchmark and validation of the Effective Bethe Ansatz (EBA) method for approximating non-integrable spin chains by deforming exact Bethe wavefunctions from integrable points.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the recently proposed Effective Bethe Ansatz method for studying non-integrable quantum spin chains, particularly to understand its scope and limitations for low-energy physics.
AI.method: Applied the EBA method to the non-integrable regime of the spin-1 bilinear-biquadratic chain, starting from two integrable endpoints (Takhtajan-Babujian point and Lai-Sutherland point). Validation was performed through direct comparison with exact diagonalization, assessing energy, fidelity, and entanglement entropy.
AI.result: EBA provides physically accurate description near integrability with fidelity decreasing controllably as perturbation increases. The method successfully captures finite-size effects like level crossings (manifested as sharp fidelity drops) and probes potential phase transitions.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes EBA as a reliable and efficient semi-analytical tool for studying low-energy physics in non-integrable quantum spin chains, clarifying its scope and limitations.

title: Quantum Algebraic Diversity: Single-Copy Density Matrix Estimation via Group-Structured Measurements
summary: We extend the algebraic diversity (AD) framework from classical signal processing to quantum measurement theory. The central result -- the Quantum Algebraic Diversity (QAD) Theorem -- establishes that a group-structured positive operator-valued measure (POVM) applied to a single copy of a quantum state produces a group-averaged density matrix estimator that recovers the spectral structure of the true density matrix, analogous to the classical result that a group-averaged outer product recovers covariance eigenstructure from a single observation.
